senses.definition: the process of increasing the carbon content of a metal (usually steel) by introducing carbon into its surface or interior, typically through heating in a carbon-rich environment
examples: Recarburization improves surface hardness in carburized steel components.
Industrial furnaces control temperature and atmosphere to achieve effective recarburization.

senses.definition: to restore or increase the carbon content of a metal, especially steel, by heating it in a carbon-rich environment
examples: The factory plans to recarburize the steel to enhance its wear resistance.
After initial forging, the metal was recarburized to achieve the desired hardness.

senses.definition: a device or substance used to recarburize metal, introducing carbon into its surface or interior during heat treatment
examples: The recarburizer introduced a controlled amount of carbon during the process.
Different grades of recarburizers affect carbon diffusion rates.

senses.definition: to convert or transform something to a different form of soul, essence, or character; not a standard technical term; may appear in creative or speculative contexts
examples: In the novel, the alchemist seeks to recarnify the essence of the metal.
The artist attempted to recarnify the material into a living sculpture.

senses.definition: to cover or line again with carpet
examples: We decided to recarpet the stairs to give the hallway a fresh look.
The crew will recarpet the living room before the next sale.

senses.definition: covered again with carpet; having undergone recarpeting
examples: The recarpeted stairs looked brand new.
We bought a recarpeted rug to match the updated living room.

senses.definition: the act or process of laying carpet again; the action of recarpeting
examples: Recarpeting is scheduled for the weekend.
The hotel renovated with new recarpeting throughout the lobby.

senses.definition: plural of recarpet; multiple instances of recarpeting tasks or areas
examples: The recarpets in the hallway and bedrooms were replaced.
Different rooms require different recarpets during renovation.

senses.definition: the act of carrying again or a subsequent carriage; also a term in transportation or childbirth context for medical care after delivery
examples: The horse's recarriage of the load was carefully planned.
In medical terms, recarriage can refer to the resettling of the baby after birth in some older texts.

senses.definition: carried again; transported a second time
examples: The recarried load was placed onto the truck.
After a brief pause, the crates were recarried to the dock.

senses.definition: a device or person that carries again or reintroduces something to a carrier; may appear in specialized contexts
examples: The recarrier system redistributes goods to avoid bottlenecks.
In some industries, a recarrier is used to move cargo between modes.

senses.definition: third-person singular of recarry; to carry again
examples: He recarries the package to the truck.
The team recarries equipment for the next shift.

senses.definition: to carry again; to transport again
examples: We will recarry the crates to the warehouse.
The crate was recarried after inspection.

senses.definition: present participle of recarry; carrying again
examples: They are recarrying the equipment to the ship.
Recarrying the pallets takes extra time.

senses.definition: to cart again; to recart a project or decoration; also a term used in art setups to reframe or reframe components
examples: They decided to recart the stage with new props.
We will recart the layout to improve visibility.

senses.definition: to carve again or carve anew; to revise relief or sculpture
examples: The statue was recarved to add finer details.
They decided to recarve the wooden panel for a cleaner look.

senses.definition: having been carved again; newly carved
examples: The recarved wooden panel gleamed in the light.
They displayed the recarved statue at the gallery.

senses.definition: the act of carving again; the process of re-sculpting
examples: The recarving of the figure took several days.
Recarving revealed new details in the relief.

senses.definition: to put something in a new case or container; to set in a different housing
examples: They recased the electronics to improve protection.
The artifacts were recased for display.

senses.definition: to exchange or convert cash again; to convert into cash; to re-cash a check or instrument
examples: The teller will recash the check after verification.
We need to recash the old bonds for liquidity.

senses.definition: to place again in a casket or container; to rewrap or rebox for storage or transport
examples: They will recasket the valuables for secure transport.
The relics were recasketed before the exhi bition.

senses.definition: to give a new form or meaning to something; to remake or restructure
examples: The company decided to recast its strategy for the new market.
The tale was recast as a modern thriller.

senses.definition: a device or person that recasts; one who remakes or reshapes
examples: The recaster redesigned the product line.
As a recaster, she shifted the narrative to a new audience.

senses.definition: the act of giving something a new form or shaping again; a remake
examples: The recasting of the cast brought fresh energy to the film.
Recasting the project required input from multiple departments.

senses.definition: plural of recast; instances of giving a new form or remake
examples: The director announced two recasts for the leading roles.
Recasts in the project schedule caused delays.

senses.definition: a person or tool that recasts; one who reshapes or remakes
examples: The recaster redesigned the component.
A skilled recaster can adapt a product for new markets.

senses.definition: the act of recasting; the process of remaking or reshaping
examples: The recasting of the narrative improved coherence.
A period of recasting followed the initial prototype.

senses.definition: plural of recast; multiple remakings or reshapes
examples: Several recasts were proposed during editing.
The recasts altered the character dynamics.
